skill.name: vercel-composition-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for React composition patterns that reduce boolean props, promote compound components, and keep shared state in providers.
skill.summary: Apply scalable React composition patterns for cleaner component APIs.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are documentation false positives. Backtick hits are Markdown inline code and fenced TSX examples, weak-crypto hits are impact words such as HIGH and CRITICAL, and network hits are React documentation links. No executable code, prompt injection attempt, data exfiltration, or unsafe command behavior was found.

content.value_statement: React components with many boolean props become hard to maintain and review. This skill gives agents clear patterns for compound components, explicit variants, and provider-based state.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to replace boolean prop modes with explicit React component variants
Shows compound component structures that share state through context
Defines provider patterns with state, actions, and meta interfaces
Guides lifting state so sibling UI can read and submit shared data
Compares children composition with render prop usage for component APIs
Provides incorrect and correct TSX examples for each pattern
limitations: Provides documentation and examples only; it does not run code or inspect a repository by itself
Focuses on React and TypeScript component architecture, not backend design
Examples use simplified component names and may need adaptation to a design system
Does not replace product-specific accessibility, performance, or security review

use_cases.title: Refactor Prop-Heavy Components
use_cases.description: Convert components with several boolean modes into explicit variants that compose shared internals.

use_cases.title: Design Component Library APIs
use_cases.description: Create compound component APIs that allow consumers to choose the pieces they need.
use_cases.target_user: Design system and UI library authors

use_cases.title: Review React Architecture
use_cases.description: Check whether state ownership, context boundaries, and composition choices are clear and maintainable.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Boolean Props
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vercel-composition-patterns skill to review this React component. Identify boolean props that create hidden modes and suggest explicit variants.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Compound Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vercel-composition-patterns skill to design a compound component API for this UI. Include the provider, frame, input, footer, and action pieces.

prompt_templates.title: Lift Shared State
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vercel-composition-patterns skill to move this component state into a provider. Define the state, actions, and meta interface.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vercel-composition-patterns skill to refactor this React feature. Replace boolean modes, compose children, lift shared state, and explain tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: A composer component has isThread, isEditing, isForwarding, and showFormatting props.
output: The response recommends separate ThreadComposer, EditComposer, and ForwardComposer variants.
Shared UI is moved into Composer.Frame, Composer.Input, Composer.Footer, and action subcomponents.
Conditional branches become explicit composition at the call site.

output_examples.input: A dialog needs a preview and submit button outside the composer box.
output: The response recommends a provider around the dialog, not state trapped inside the composer.
MessagePreview reads state from context, and the external submit button uses the provider actions.
The visual layout can change without breaking the state boundary.

output_examples.input: A component accepts renderHeader, renderFooter, and renderActions callbacks.
output: The response checks whether children composition is simpler for the static structure.
Render props are kept only when the parent must pass item data or state back to the child.
The final API is easier to scan and compose in JSX.

best_practices: Create explicit variants when a component has multiple behavior modes.
Keep state management inside providers and expose a small context interface.
Use children composition for static structure and render props only when data must flow back.
anti_patterns: Adding more boolean props to a component that already has mode-specific behavior.
Using effects or refs to copy state upward when a provider boundary would be clearer.
Building monolithic components with render callbacks for every internal region.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps agents design React components with composition instead of many boolean configuration props.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready components?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples. Generated code still needs project-specific review and testing.

faq.question: When should I use compound components?
faq.answer: Use them when consumers need flexible control over related subcomponents that share state or behavior.

faq.question: How does the provider pattern help?
faq.answer: A provider lets components inside the boundary share state and actions without prop drilling.

faq.question: Are render props always discouraged?
faq.answer: No. Render props are useful when the parent must provide data, such as list items or indexes.

faq.question: Can this work with an existing design system?
faq.answer: Yes. The patterns can wrap existing inputs, buttons, and layout components from a design system.
